does: This paper conducts a systematic comparative analysis of four major open-source LLM vulnerability scanners—Garak, Giskard, PyRIT, and CyberSecEval—evaluating their capabilities to detect security risks like information leakage and jailbreak attacks. The authors examine each tool's distinctive features, design principles, and practical usability, then perform quantitative benchmarking to assess detection performance. A key finding is that these scanners suffer from significant reliability issues in consistently identifying successful attacks, revealing a fundamental gap in current red-teaming capabilities. The study contributes a preliminary labeled dataset to help address this detection gap and provides strategic recommendations for organizations selecting scanners based on customizability, test comprehensiveness, and industry specifications.
connects: This work builds on the growing field of AI safety and LLM security, which has increasingly adopted red-teaming methodologies from cybersecurity to systematically expose model vulnerabilities. It extends prior efforts to catalog attack vectors against LLMs—including prompt injection, information disclosure, and jailbreaks—by taking a meta-analytical view of the tools designed to detect them. The paper responds to the emergence of open-source vulnerability scanning frameworks that attempt to operationalize adversarial testing practices for language models, synthesizing insights across multiple independent tool development efforts that have each tackled LLM robustness assessment from different angles.
enables: By identifying and documenting reliability gaps in current scanners, this work enables development of more robust red-teaming tools and benchmarks for LLM security evaluation. The preliminary labeled dataset provides foundation material for building improved detection methods and training more reliable vulnerability classifiers. The comparative framework and strategic recommendations support the standardization of LLM security assessment practices across industry, facilitating adoption of best practices. Future work can build on these findings to develop unified testing methodologies, more comprehensive attack coverage, and better metrics for assessing LLM resilience, ultimately supporting deployment of more trustworthy conversational AI systems in high-stakes applications.
